input.background: In all-electric homes, the amount of electricity expended is of interest to consumers, builders, and groups involved with energy conservation. Suppose we wish to investigate the monthly electrical usage, y, in all-electric homes and its relationship to the size, x, of the home. Moreover, suppose we think that monthly electrical usage in all-electric homes is related to the size of the home by the quadratic model y=β0+β1x+β2x^2+ε. To fit the model, the values of y and x are collected for 15 homes during a particular month. The data are shown in Table 12.2.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline Table 12.2 & Home Size-Electrical Usage Data \\
\hline Size of Home, x (sq. ft.) & Monthly Usage, $y$ (kilowatt-hours) \\
\hline 1,290 & 1,182 \\
\hline 1,350 & 1,172 \\
\hline 1,470 & 1,264 \\
\hline 1,600 & 1,493 \\
\hline 1,710 & 1,571 \\
\hline 1,840 & 1,711 \\
\hline 1,980 & 1,804 \\
\hline 2,230 & 1,840 \\
\hline 2,400 & 1,986 \\
\hline 2,710 & 2,007 \\
\hline 2,930 & 1,984 \\
\hline 3,000 & 1,960 \\
\hline 3,210 & 2,001 \\
\hline 3,240 & 1,928 \\
\hline 3,520 & 1,945 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: Use the method of least squares to estimate the unknown parameters β0, β1, and β2 in the quadratic model.

output.answer: We used Excel to fit the model to the data in Table 12.2. Part of the Excel regression output is displayed in Figure 12.14. The least squares estimates of the β parameters (highlighted) are β̂0=-806.7, β̂1=1.9616, and β̂2=-.00034. Therefore, the equation that minimizes the SSE for the data is ŷ=-806.7+1.9616x-.00034x^2.
